steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the underlined sentence ("Does the concept of culture encompass knowledge, or laws, or both these things and others?") does in the passage. The surrounding text: before it, the author calls "culture" a hazy concept; after it, the author says haziness is fine for casual use but sociology needs standardization to progress.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'It lists two essential components of what is referred to by Bonnell and Hunt as "culture."': The sentence poses a question about what "culture" might include — it does not list confirmed components, and it is the author's question, not Bonnell and Hunt's claim. ✗
  • Choice B — 'It presents a point regarding the definition of "culture" on which nonspecialists and specialists tend to disagree.': The passage never frames this as a specialists-vs-nonspecialists disagreement. The sentence illustrates vagueness, not a group conflict. ✗
  • Choice C — 'It describes the questions first raised by Bonnell and Hunt's inquiry into the meaning of the word "culture."': Bonnell and Hunt are cited only for noting the concept's vagueness; this rhetorical question is the author's own illustration, not a product of their inquiry. ✗
  • Choice D — 'It illustrates a difficulty associated with the meaning of "culture" that the author believes holds back an academic field.': The question dramatizes the concept's haziness, and the very next sentence states that this haziness prevents sociology from truly progressing — exactly what D describes. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
